title: Madeira cake
subtitle: Classic British lemon butter cake
excerpt: The plainest and most reliable of British cakes: butter, sugar, eggs, flour, and lemon zest, creamed together and baked slowly in a loaf tin. The candied peel laid on top halfway through baking is traditional. Dense and fine-crumbed, suitable for serving plain or with afternoon tea.
sourceNotes: Madeira cake is a documented British cake tradition, named not for the island of Madeira but for the wine with which it was traditionally served. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the Madeira cake recipe documents a creamed butter sponge with lemon rind and a strip of citron or candied peel laid across the top before baking, noting that the cake should be baked slowly to produce a fine, close crumb. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: the plain cake chapter documents the Madeira cake as a standard British household cake made by the creaming method, with lemon zest as the sole flavouring.

content.text: Madeira cake is what butter, eggs, flour, and sugar can be when they are treated correctly: not a vehicle for chocolate or fruit, just themselves. The creaming method is everything, beat the butter and sugar long enough and the crumb will be fine, close, and moist. Rush it and the crumb is coarse and the texture grainy. The lemon zest is the only flavouring.

items.name: Unsalted butter
items.prepNote: softened, leave out for 1 hour if needed

content.text: Preheat the oven to 165°C fan. Butter and line the loaf tin. The

starts here: beat the {{unsalted-butter}} and {{caster-sugar}} together until the mixture is very pale, fluffy, and noticeably increased in volume, at least 4 to 5 minutes with a hand mixer, 7 to 8 minutes by hand. Do not rush this step; the air beaten in here is what leavens the cake.

Beat in the {{eggs}} one at a time, adding a tablespoon of the flour after the second egg to prevent curdling. Beat in the {{vanilla-extract}} and the zest from the {{lemon}}. Fold in the remaining sifted {{plain-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} in two additions until just combined. Spoon into the lined tin and level the top.

Bake for 30 minutes, then lay the {{mixed-peel}} in a row along the centre of the cake if using. Continue baking for a further 20 to 25 minutes until the cake is pale gold, pulling away from the edges, and a skewer comes out clean. The top should have a slight crack along the length, this is characteristic. Cool in the tin for 15 minutes before turning out.
